- c (dark gray): pixels=72, bbox=[18,3]-[29,17] - dark gray moved to [18,3]-[29,17]
- Y (yellow): pixels=72, bbox=[42,3]-[53,17] - yellow L at [42,3]-[53,17]
The dark gray is now at [18,3]-[29,17], which has the same column range as the yellow L (cols 3-17). The dark gray is 12 rows tall (rows 18-29), and the yellow L is 12 rows tall (rows 42-53).
So MOUSE(47,10) moved the dark gray to the same column range as the yellow L, but the dark gray is still at rows 18-29, while the yellow L is at rows 42-53.
The dark gray needs to move down by 24 rows to overlap with the yellow L.
Looking at the Level 1 solution, after MOUSE(49,55), the next action was LEFT 16. This moved the divider left, which might have moved the dark gray down.
Let me try LEFT to see if it moves the dark gray down.
Actually, in Level 1, the sequence was RIGHT 12, DOWN 10, SPACE, MOUSE(49,55), LEFT 16. Let me try a similar sequence in Level 2.

In Level 2, after MOUSE(47,10), the dark gray is at [18,3]-[29,17], which is above the yellow L at [42,3]-[53,17].

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Dark gray at [18,3]-[29,17], yellow L at [42,3]-[53,17]. Same columns but different rows. Let me try LEFT to move dark gray down.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT to move dark gray down
action(['LEFT']*12)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT 12:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 10 and n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y', 'S']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
print("Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Game over?", last_action_result.get('game_over'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
